text: <par>This form is from *Western* Tibetan, not Written Tibetan. JAM checked it in Jaeschke. The language field should be updated to reflect this.</par>
<par>Jäschke has “also knuckle ni f.” . “ni f.” =<emph>ni fallor</emph> ‘if I am not mistaken’ .</par>
<par>1st syll. was tagged with now-defunct #279 <latinform>kur</latinform> JAW/CHEEK.</par>

<par>WT generally preserves final <latinform>*-l</latinform> as such, though there are several roots which show final <latinform>-l ~ -n</latinform> alteration, and a couple where the final has simply developed into <latinform>-n</latinform>. See STC p. 15, note 53.</par>

<par>WT <latinform>rgód</latinform> may mean "wild" (Jäschke 81:104), cf. <latinform>byɑ-rgód</latinform> "vulture, bird of prey"; <latinform>mdzo-rgód</latinform> "cattle (wild)"; <latinform>pʻɑg-rgód</latinform> "boar (wild)", <latinform>rŋɑ-rgód</latinform> "camel (wild)"; <latinform>rɑ-rgód</latinform> "goat (wild)"; and <latinform>ɣyɑg-rgód</latinform> "yak (wild) / ox (wild)".</par>
<par>The first two syllables are a loan from Indo-Aryan, cf. <emph>mahiṣá</emph> 'buffalo' (Turner 1962:573).</par>

<par>The cognacy of WT <latinform>śa</latinform> is not certain, since WT does preserve final <reconstruction>*-l</reconstruction>. The first syllable is homophonous with <latinform>śa</latinform> (< <xref ref="34">#34</xref>), though Jäschke (p.556) does not include it under that lemma, evidently considering it to be a separate morpheme. The Newar form <latinform>saː</latinform> 'placenta' apparently means "house", but Newar <latinform>swa̩</latinform> 'nest' seems to be a genuine reflex of <reconstruction>*tsywal</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>These WT forms reflect the allofam <latinform>*dzyow</latinform> (see, HPTB:224, 382).</par>
<par>This form is glossed 'wing; petal, leaf, fan' by Csoma de Kőrös 1834.</par>

<par>WT <latinform>dpun̊-pa rkan̊</latinform> also means 'upper arm', but is to be analyzed as "shoulder's leg" (<latinform>dpun̊-pa</latinform> 'shoulder'). Perhaps the girth of the upper arm reminded speakers of a leg.</par>
